title: The ethics of accepting freebies as a journalist
summary: Argue whether journalists can accept free meals, trips, or products from companies they cover, and what this means for editorial independence.
topic: The ethics of accepting freebies as a journalist
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) examining whether journalists are ethically justified in accepting free meals, press trips, review products, or other gifts from organisations they cover. Consider what this means for editorial independence, public trust, and the commercial realities of media organisations. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete example. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What makes the freebie question genuinely difficult is not the obvious case — a journalist taking a…
Admittedly, most experienced journalists would insist that a free press trip to…
Having attended dozens of press junkets over a career, a travel writer may find it…

bản sao để đánh giá (sản phẩm được gửi miễn phí để phóng viên đánh giá)
làm tổn hại đến tính chính trực
niềm tin của công chúng
mối quan hệ giữ khoảng cách / không thiên vị
tuyên bố công khai / thông báo minh bạch
áp lực thương mại
xây dựng / duy trì quyền tiếp cận
đưa tin thiên vị / có lợi
ngân sách của tòa soạn
vẻ bề ngoài của sự không phù hợp / không đúng mực
hướng dẫn đạo đức
mang ơn / lệ thuộc vào
sự mềm mỏng trong tiềm thức

hintVocab.phrase: to send a signal to the industry
hintVocab.meaningVi: gửi tín hiệu đến ngành
tự quản lý / tự điều chỉnh

sampleText:
Journalism's claim to public trust rests on a simple proposition: the journalist's loyalty is to the reader, not to the subject being covered. What accepting a free hotel stay, a business-class flight, or a case of wine from a winery under review does to this proposition is not destroy it immediately — it is, rather, to introduce a doubt that is difficult to dislodge.

The psychological mechanism is well-established. Reciprocity bias — the tendency to feel obligated toward those who have given us something — operates below the level of conscious intention. A journalist who has been flown to Bordeaux at a vineyard's expense and accommodated in its guest house is not necessarily going to file a glowing review. But they are, on average, going to find it harder to write a negative one. This is not a hypothesis. Research into pharmaceutical advertising has demonstrated that even small gifts — a branded pen, a free lunch — shift prescribing behaviour among doctors who sincerely believe their clinical judgement is unaffected. The same mechanism does not disappear when the recipients are journalists rather than physicians.

Admittedly, many media organisations operate on budgets that make self-funded reporting difficult or impossible. Travel journalism in particular has long relied on hosted trips — not because editors are corrupt but because the alternative is simply not covering a destination at all. A small magazine with three staff members cannot afford to send a writer to Kyoto for a week. The choice, in practice, is often between a hosted trip and no trip.

Even so, the structural argument for accepting freebies does not neutralise the ethical problem; it merely explains why the problem persists. Accepted uncritically, the press junket model means that coverage of travel destinations, new cars, and consumer electronics is systematically skewed toward products whose manufacturers can afford to host journalists. Emerging destinations, independent producers, and budget products are underrepresented not because they lack merit but because no one is paying for the flights.

Granted, a transparency disclosure — noting that a trip was hosted or a product was supplied — is better than silence. Some publications now require it. But disclosure is a mitigation, not a solution. Having been wined and dined by a company, a journalist cannot simply neutralise that experience by appending a footnote. The more honest position is to pay your own way when you can, return gifts when you cannot, and treat the press junket model not as a perk of the profession but as a structural failure that skews coverage in ways readers cannot always see.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Extended what-cleft to isolate a complex subject
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What accepting a free hotel stay, a business-class flight, or a case of wine from a winery under review does to this proposition is not destroy it immediately' là một câu chẻ đôi có chủ ngữ rất dài. Cấu trúc 'What [gerund phrase] does to X is [infinitive]' cho phép tác giả đặt toàn bộ hành động phức tạp làm chủ đề logic mà không phá vỡ nhịp điệu câu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What accepting a free hotel stay, a business-class flight, or a case of wine from a winery under review does to this proposition is not destroy it immediately' is a what-cleft with an extended gerund subject. The structure 'What [gerund phrase] does to X is [infinitive]' allows the writer to foreground a complex action as the logical topic without breaking sentence rhythm.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Fronted perfect participle clause to signal a logical consequence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having been wined and dined by a company, a journalist cannot simply neutralise that experience by appending a footnote' dùng mệnh đề phân từ hoàn thành bị động ('Having been wined and dined') để mở câu. Điều này nhấn mạnh rằng trải nghiệm đã xảy ra trước và hậu quả của nó không thể bị xóa bỏ — mối quan hệ trước-sau (anterior) được nén vào mệnh đề mở đầu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having been wined and dined by a company, a journalist cannot simply neutralise that experience by appending a footnote' opens with a perfect passive participle clause. This foregrounds the prior experience and its irreversibility — the anterior relationship (X happened first, therefore Y follows) is compressed into the opening phrase.

sampleTranslationVi:
Yêu cầu về niềm tin của công chúng của báo chí dựa trên một đề xuất đơn giản: lòng trung thành của nhà báo là với độc giả, không phải với đối tượng được đưa tin. Điều mà việc chấp nhận kỳ nghỉ khách sạn miễn phí, vé máy bay hạng thương gia hoặc thùng rượu từ một nhà máy rượu đang được đánh giá làm với đề xuất này không phải là phá hủy nó ngay lập tức — mà là tạo ra một nghi ngờ khó xóa bỏ.

Cơ chế tâm lý đã được thiết lập rõ ràng. Thiên kiến có đi có lại — xu hướng cảm thấy có nghĩa vụ với những người đã cho mình thứ gì đó — hoạt động dưới mức ý thức có chủ ý. Một nhà báo đã được bay đến Bordeaux với chi phí của vườn nho và được đón tiếp tại nhà khách của nó không nhất thiết sẽ nộp một bài đánh giá tích cực. Nhưng tính trung bình, họ sẽ thấy khó khăn hơn khi viết một bài tiêu cực. Đây không phải là một giả thuyết. Nghiên cứu về quảng cáo dược phẩm đã chứng minh rằng ngay cả những món quà nhỏ — một chiếc bút có thương hiệu, một bữa trưa miễn phí — cũng thay đổi hành vi kê đơn của các bác sĩ thực sự tin rằng phán đoán lâm sàng của họ không bị ảnh hưởng. Cơ chế tương tự không biến mất khi người nhận là nhà báo chứ không phải bác sĩ.

Thừa nhận rằng, nhiều tổ chức truyền thông hoạt động với ngân sách khiến việc đưa tin tự tài trợ trở nên khó khăn hoặc không thể. Báo chí du lịch đặc biệt từ lâu đã dựa vào các chuyến đi được tài trợ — không phải vì các biên tập viên tham nhũng mà vì thay thế đơn giản là không đưa tin về một điểm đến nào cả. Một tạp chí nhỏ với ba nhân viên không thể đủ khả năng gửi một nhà văn đến Kyoto trong một tuần. Sự lựa chọn, trong thực tế, thường là giữa một chuyến đi được tài trợ và không có chuyến đi nào.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận cấu trúc để chấp nhận đồ miễn phí không loại bỏ vấn đề đạo đức; nó chỉ giải thích tại sao vấn đề tiếp tục tồn tại. Được chấp nhận một cách không phê phán, mô hình chuyến đi báo chí có nghĩa là tin tức về các điểm đến du lịch, xe mới và điện tử tiêu dùng bị lệch lạc một cách hệ thống về phía các sản phẩm mà nhà sản xuất có thể đủ khả năng tiếp đón nhà báo. Các điểm đến mới nổi, nhà sản xuất độc lập và sản phẩm ngân sách bị đại diện không đủ không phải vì chúng thiếu giá trị mà vì không ai trả tiền cho các chuyến bay.

Thừa nhận rằng, một tuyên bố công khai — lưu ý rằng một chuyến đi được tài trợ hoặc một sản phẩm được cung cấp — tốt hơn là im lặng. Một số ấn phẩm hiện yêu cầu điều đó. Nhưng công khai là một biện pháp giảm nhẹ, không phải là giải pháp. Đã được một công ty chiêu đãi ăn uống, một nhà báo không thể đơn giản vô hiệu hóa trải nghiệm đó bằng cách thêm một chú thích. Lập trường thành thật hơn là tự trả tiền khi có thể, trả lại quà khi không thể, và đối xử với mô hình chuyến đi báo chí không phải như một đặc quyền của nghề mà như một thất bại cấu trúc làm lệch tin tức theo những cách mà độc giả không phải lúc nào cũng nhìn thấy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to foreground a complex action as subject?
options: What accepting a free hotel stay, a business-class flight, or a case of wine does to this proposition is not destroy it immediately.
Accepting a freebie does not immediately destroy journalism's credibility but introduces doubt.
A free hotel stay or flight, when accepted, introduces a doubt that is difficult to dislodge into journalism.
Journalism's credibility is what accepting free hotels and flights does not immediately destroy.
mcQuestions.explanation: The what-cleft 'What [gerund phrase] does to X is [infinitive]' foregrounds the act of accepting freebies as the logical subject, placing the key claim after 'is'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What [action] does to X is Y' — not Y first, and not the action buried in the middle.

options: A journalist's preference for publishing articles about brands that advertise in their newspaper
The tendency to feel unconsciously obligated toward people who have given you something
A bias in favour of publishing both sides of a story equally
The tendency for journalists to copy each other's stories
mcQuestions.explanation: Reciprocity bias is a documented psychological tendency: receiving a gift or favour creates a subconscious sense of obligation to return it — even when the recipient believes they are being objective.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Reciprocity' means giving back in return — what kind of psychological pull does receiving a gift create?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly backgrounds the agent?
options: A vineyard flew the journalist to Bordeaux and accommodated them in its guest house.
Bordeaux's vineyard accommodated and flew the journalist there at its own expense.
The journalist who has been flown to Bordeaux at a vineyard's expense and accommodated in its guest house finds it harder to write a negative review.
The journalist flew to Bordeaux, having been hosted by a vineyard in its guest house.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who has been flown to Bordeaux' and 'accommodated in its guest house' use passive constructions to foreground the journalist's experience, backgrounding the vineyard's role as host.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence makes the journalist the subject while describing actions done to them?

options: A publisher's right to produce books without government approval
The legal right for editors to correct factual errors after publication
The practice of assigning different stories to different editors
A journalist's or news organisation's freedom to report and publish based on news values, not commercial or political pressure
mcQuestions.explanation: Editorial independence means that news decisions — what to report, how to frame it, what to publish — are made on journalistic grounds, not influenced by advertisers, governments, or other outside parties.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'editorial' (relating to editing and publishing content) + 'independence' — independence from what?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a fronted perfect participle clause?
options: Having been wined and dined by a company, a journalist cannot simply neutralise that experience by appending a footnote.
A journalist cannot neutralise their experience by appending a footnote, having been wined and dined.
A journalist, having been wined and dined by a company, which they cannot neutralise by appending a footnote.
By a company, having been wined and dined, a journalist cannot simply neutralise that experience.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having been wined and dined by a company' opens the sentence as a fronted perfect passive participle clause, establishing prior cause before the main clause consequence.
mcQuestions.hint: The fronted participle clause comes before the main subject and establishes something that happened first.

options: A negative term for sensationalist journalism
A free trip or event organised by a company specifically to generate favourable media coverage
A press conference held by a government minister
An investigative journalism project funded by a charitable foundation
mcQuestions.explanation: A press junket is a promotional trip or event paid for by a company and attended by journalists, typically to encourage positive coverage of a product, destination, or release.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of who pays for the trip and why they are paying for it.

options: Many media organisations operate on budgets admittedly that make self-funded reporting difficult or impossible.
Admittedly budgets of many media organisations are making self-funded reporting difficult admittedly.
Admittedly, many media organisations operate on budgets that make self-funded reporting difficult or impossible.
Self-funded reporting is difficult admittedly impossible many media organisations operate on budgets.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' is followed by a complete independent clause with normal word order — the standard form of a concession marker.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'Admittedly,', the clause should have a normal subject-verb order.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'disclosure is a mitigation, not a solution' mean?
options: Disclosing a conflict of interest eliminates the ethical problem entirely
Media organisations should disclose conflicts of interest only when they are serious
Disclosure of information should be treated as equivalent to finding a solution
Telling readers about a freebie reduces but does not remove the ethical problem it creates
mcQuestions.explanation: A mitigation reduces harm without eliminating it. Disclosing that a trip was hosted makes the conflict visible but does not undo the psychological effects of having received the hospitality.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Mitigation' is a reduction, not a removal — what is being reduced but not removed here?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'coverage is systematically skewed' mean?
options: Journalism is regularly and structurally biased in a particular direction due to underlying incentives
News reports are consistently formatted using the same visual template
Journalists deliberately choose to cover stories that interest them personally
Coverage of certain topics is restricted by government regulation
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Systematically' means not randomly but as a result of underlying structure; 'skewed' means tilted or biased. Together they describe a structural, not incidental, distortion in coverage.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Skewed' means angled or biased in a particular direction — 'systematically' means this happens as a pattern, not by accident.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from a concession to a rebuttal?
options: For instance, travel magazines often rely on hosted trips to cover exotic destinations.
Even so, the structural argument for accepting freebies does not neutralise the ethical problem.
Similarly, other professions such as medicine also face pressures from commercial interests.
In addition, transparency disclosures are increasingly required by major news organisations.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the weight of the concession and then reasserts the main argument — the conceded point is real but does not defeat the position.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option says 'the concession is valid, but my argument still holds'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the appearance of impropriety' mean?
options: The actual evidence of a journalist having been bribed
The physical appearance of unprofessional behaviour during an interview
The impression that something unethical may have occurred, even if it has not been proven
A legal charge brought against a journalist for misconduct
mcQuestions.explanation: The appearance of impropriety means that even if no actual wrongdoing has occurred, the situation looks questionable — and that appearance can itself damage public trust.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Appearance' here means how something looks to others — does the situation look improper, even if nothing improper actually happened?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Obviously, taking free stuff from companies is a conflict of interest and most journalists know it's dodgy.
Freebies make it really hard for journalists to be fair and basically everyone knows that.
Getting free trips and meals from companies can kind of change how journalists write about them, which is not great.
The acceptance of hospitality from organisations under journalistic scrutiny raises documented concerns about cognitive bias and editorial independence.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The acceptance of hospitality', 'organisations under journalistic scrutiny', 'documented concerns', 'cognitive bias', and 'editorial independence' are precise, formal collocations appropriate to academic argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'obviously', 'basically', 'kind of', 'dodgy', and 'not great' — these are informal.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be beholden to' someone mean?
options: To be indebted or under an obligation to someone in a way that affects your independence
To legally owe money to a creditor
To hold strong admiration for a respected colleague
To be contractually bound to a company through an employment agreement
mcQuestions.explanation: To be beholden to someone means to owe them something — typically gratitude or favours — in a way that makes you feel obligated and potentially less independent.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'behold' as 'to hold onto' — if you owe someone, you are held by that obligation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses inversion correctly after a negative adverb?
options: A journalist should never accept gifts from companies they are covering without full disclosure.
Never should a journalist accept gifts from companies they are covering without full disclosure.
Never a journalist should accept gifts from companies they are covering without full disclosure.
A journalist never should accept gifts without full disclosure from companies they are covering.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Never', the auxiliary ('should') must precede the subject ('a journalist') — standard subject-auxiliary inversion after a negative adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'Never', does the auxiliary come before or after the subject?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'arms-length relationship' mean in journalism?
options: A relationship that involves physical distance between interviewer and subject
A formal agreement not to quote a source directly
A relationship in which a journalist maintains enough distance and independence from a source to report on them objectively
A relationship conducted exclusively by email rather than in person
mcQuestions.explanation: An arms-length relationship means keeping enough distance to avoid bias or dependence — the journalist can cover the subject without feeling obligated to them.
mcQuestions.hint: An arm's length is far enough away that you cannot be grabbed — what kind of professional distance does this suggest?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best paraphrases 'not because they lack merit but because no one is paying for the flights'?
options: Small producers are underreported because journalists prefer famous brands.
Budget products are not covered because readers are not interested in them.
Emerging destinations are avoided by journalists who find them too difficult to report on.
Independent producers receive less coverage not due to inferior quality but because they cannot afford to host press trips.
mcQuestions.explanation: The original sentence argues that structural funding — who can afford to host journalists — drives coverage, not the quality of the product or destination being covered.
mcQuestions.hint: The key contrast is between 'merit' (quality) and 'paying for flights' (ability to fund press access).

criticalThinkingQuestion: If you read a glowing review of a hotel and then discovered the journalist stayed there for free, would it change how much you trusted the review? Does your answer change if the review is negative rather than positive? What does this tell you about the direction of the bias?
